concepts.name: Heterotrophic Nutrition
concepts.description: A mode of nutrition where organisms obtain food by consuming other organisms, as they cannot produce their own.

concepts.name: Holozoic Nutrition
concepts.description: A specific type of heterotrophic nutrition involving ingestion, digestion, absorption, assimilation, and egestion of solid or liquid food.

concepts.name: Digestive System
concepts.description: The organ system in the human body responsible for breaking down food into nutrients that the body can use.

concepts.name: Alimentary Canal
concepts.description: The long, muscular tube that extends from the mouth to the anus, through which food passes and is digested.

concepts.name: Ingestion
concepts.description: The process of taking food into the body through the mouth.

concepts.name: Mouth & Salivary Glands
concepts.description: The initial part of the alimentary canal where food enters, and glands that secrete saliva to begin digestion.

concepts.name: Salivary Amylase
concepts.description: An enzyme found in saliva that begins the digestion of complex carbohydrates (starch) into simpler sugars.

concepts.name: Esophagus (Food Pipe)
concepts.description: A muscular tube that transports food from the pharynx to the stomach through peristaltic movements.

concepts.name: Stomach & Gastric Juice
concepts.description: A J-shaped muscular organ where food is stored and initial protein digestion begins, aided by acidic secretions.

concepts.name: Pepsin
concepts.description: An enzyme present in gastric juice that initiates the breakdown of proteins into smaller peptides in the stomach.

concepts.name: Hydrochloric Acid (HCl)
concepts.description: A strong acid secreted in the stomach that creates an acidic environment for pepsin activation and kills harmful bacteria.

concepts.name: Mucus
concepts.description: A protective layer secreted by the stomach lining to shield it from the corrosive action of hydrochloric acid.

concepts.name: Liver & Pancreas (Accessory Glands)
concepts.description: Organs that produce digestive juices (bile from liver, pancreatic juice from pancreas) and enzymes, which are released into the small intestine to aid digestion.

concepts.name: Small Intestine (Major Digestion & Absorption)
concepts.description: The primary site in the alimentary canal where the complete digestion of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats occurs, and most nutrient absorption takes place.

concepts.name: Large Intestine (Water Absorption)
concepts.description: The final section of the alimentary canal responsible for absorbing water and electrolytes from undigested food material, forming feces.

prerequisites.rationale: Holozoic Nutrition is introduced as a specific type of Heterotrophic Nutrition, requiring prior understanding of the general category.
The Alimentary Canal is presented as the main pathway and a key structural component within the broader Digestive System.
Ingestion is explicitly defined as the first step in the Holozoic mode of nutrition.
The Mouth is the beginning of the Alimentary Canal, making it a foundational component to understand the pathway.
Salivary Amylase is an enzyme produced by the Salivary Glands, which are located in the Mouth, establishing a direct functional dependency.
The Esophagus is the next anatomical part of the Alimentary Canal after the mouth, following the path of food.
The Stomach is the subsequent anatomical part of the Alimentary Canal after the Esophagus, continuing the food pathway.
Pepsin is an enzyme that is a key component of Gastric Juice, which is produced in the Stomach.
Hydrochloric Acid is a crucial component of Gastric Juice, which is produced in the Stomach.
Mucus is a protective component of Gastric Juice, which is produced in the Stomach.
The Small Intestine is the next major organ in the Alimentary Canal after the Stomach, where most digestion occurs.
The secretions from Liver and Pancreas (bile and pancreatic juice) are essential for the complete digestion processes that occur specifically in the Small Intestine.
The Large Intestine is the final part of the Alimentary Canal, following the Small Intestine, in the progression of food waste.
